Dublin v Galway

Possession-by-possession Analysis
29 June 2026

Dublin

Galway
1-21

Dublin won by getting on the ball more often through a superior kick-out game.

 On kick-outs, Dublin retained 70% of their own restarts to Galway’s 48%. 

How the match was won

Both teams were similarly effective at turning possession into points, but Dublin had 7 extra possessions which was enough to take the win.

1

How The Lead Changed

The score margin across the game. Galway built a 6 point lead in the second half but were immediately hauled back in a sequence of play that finished with a Dublin penalty and a black card for Liam Silke. Dublin managed to build a lead against 14 men, but Galway felt aggrieved by penalty decision that didn't go their way when the teams were level.

Above the line, Dublin lead; below the line, Galway are ahead. 

Half-time: Dublin 0-14 · Galway 0-13.

2

Spotlight: Key Phase of Play

2-05 was scored across just 8 possessions in the second half. Galway built a 6-point lead, but were hauled back immediately. Crucially, Galway's Shane Walsh was turned over inside the Dublin '45 while Galway were 5 points up. Dublin scored 1-02 off the following 3 possessions, a major shift in momentum. 

Dublin attack ↑
Galway attack ↓

DUB 1-20 – 1-20 GAL
How to read this

Reading it: The top and bottom of the graph represent each teams goal on the pitch. Each column is one possession, the chip on the halfway line shows how the ball was won, in the colour of the team that won it. The diagonal lines are kickouts coloured by the team kicking out. The bold arrow traces the attack toward goal; scores and wides line up on the goal line. A turnover or short box stretches into the next column where the opponent’s possession runs on from it.

3

The Kick-Out Battle

Each team’s own kick-outs, by where the ball was won. A filled ring is retained; a grey ring is conceded. Dublin kept 70%, Galway 48%. Galway employed an unsuccessful long kick-out strategy whereas Dublin mixed it up with 6 short kickouts leading to 5 points. 

Kick-out returns by length iNet score is the points a team scored directly from its own kick-outs minus the points conceded directly from them — counting only the possession that began with the kick-out. Short = ball won inside the own 45 m line; long = beyond it.

Scoring Chances

Every shot by location, each team attacking up its own pitch. Marker size scales with expected score; diamonds are goals, hollow rings off target. Both teams had similar success rates, but Galway gave away more scoreable frees, conceding 1-05 from placed balls.

5

Expected Points (xP)

Each shot’s expected score from an in-development On Target xP model. The difference between the teams on xP (3.7) was similar to the score margin (4 points), suggesting that shooting performance wasn't the difference between the teams.

6

Where Possession Was Lost

Turnovers conceded with each team attacking up the pitch: the opposition’s goal is at the top, their own goal at the bottom. Dublin were turned over 10 times, conceding 6 points from those possessions. Galway gave up fewer turnovers (8 times) and gave up fewer scores this way.

Dublin
Galway
Turnover led to opposition score
Conceded directly from turnovers iConceded directly from turnovers is what the opposition scored in the possession immediately after winning the ball, as goals–points (e.g. 1‑03). Only open-play turnovers — lost in midfield or in the opposition half — are counted; shots dropped short or off the post are misses, not turnovers.

Player Analysis

Tap any player to show their involvements — green for positives (scores, turnovers won, goalkeeper kick-outs won) and red for negatives (wides, shots dropped short or off the post, turnovers lost, kick-outs lost). Each team attacks up the pitch. Con O'Callaghan (#11) won the battle of the marquee forwards, outscoring Shane Walsh (#15) 1-07 to 0-01.
